summary: Survey the LLM landscape as of January 2026, from the frontier triad (GPT-5.2, Gemini 3 Pro, Claude Opus 4.5) to open models like Llama 4, Mistral Large 3, and DeepSeek V3.1. Then go deeper on the three architectural innovations powering the agentic era: reasoning models that deliberate before answering, library-scale context windows, and sparse Mixture-of-Experts efficiency. Along the way, untangle the contested meaning of 'open source AI' and what these shifts mean for your research workflow.
objectives: Identify the major frontier and open models of early 2026 and their distinguishing capabilities
Distinguish OSI-compliant open source AI from 'open weights' releases and explain the open-washing controversy
Explain how reasoning models use trained chain-of-thought and inference-time compute to solve hard problems
Describe how massive context windows change when retrieval infrastructure like RAG is actually needed
Summarize how sparse Mixture-of-Experts architectures deliver frontier intelligence at lower cost
keyTakeaways: The AI industry has moved from the Chatbot Era into the Agentic Era, where models are differentiated by reasoning capability, contextual depth, and autonomous execution rather than fluency alone.

'Open weights' models like Llama 4 are free to download but carry restrictive licenses (such as the 700-million-user cap) that fall short of the OSI's Open Source AI Definition, a practice critics call open washing.
Reasoning models are trained with reinforcement learning to produce chains of thought, letting them plan, backtrack, and verify their work instead of answering reflexively token by token.
Inference-time compute is a new scaling axis: an already-trained model can spend variable computation per query, trading speed and cost for deeper deliberation on hard problems.

Context windows have grown to library scale (up to 10 million tokens in Llama 4 Scout), so mid-sized document collections can be loaded directly into context instead of requiring RAG, though raw size matters less than how precisely a model attends to it.

Sparse Mixture-of-Experts models activate only a fraction of their total parameters per token, which is how models like Mistral Large 3 and DeepSeek V3.1 offer frontier-class performance at a fraction of the cost.

quiz.question: Meta calls Llama 4 'open source,' but the OSI and much of the technical community disagree. What is the core reason Llama's license fails the Open Source Definition?

options.text: The model weights are not actually downloadable without an API key
Meta charges a fee for any commercial use of the model
The license discriminates against specific groups, such as platforms with more than 700 million monthly active users
The model can only be run on Meta's own cloud infrastructure

quiz.explanation: The Llama Community License's 700-million-user clause violates the OSD's no-discrimination criteria, which is why such releases are more precisely called 'open weights' rather than open source.

quiz.question: Under the OSI's Open Source AI Definition (OSAID v1.0), what must a developer provide in place of the actual training data?

options.text: A complete, detailed description of the data's source, processing, and how to find it
A downloadable copy of at least 50% of the training corpus
A third-party audit certifying the data was legally obtained
Nothing—OSAID only requires the code and the trained weights

quiz.explanation: Because sharing training data is often legally impossible due to copyright, OSAID requires 'data information' detailed enough that a skilled person could understand or potentially recreate the model—a compromise critics call a loophole.

quiz.question: A reasoning model correctly answers the trick question 'A farmer has 17 sheep. All but 9 run away. How many are left?' while a standard LLM answers 8. What capability explains the difference?

options.text: The reasoning model has a larger context window, so it can see the whole question
The reasoning model was trained on more arithmetic examples
The reasoning model fact-checks its answer against a web search before responding
The reasoning model generates an internal deliberation that catches the linguistic trap instead of pattern-matching to a subtraction template

quiz.explanation: Standard LLMs operate in System 1 mode, where 'X sheep, Y run away' triggers a subtraction pattern; reasoning models produce a reasoning trace that re-reads the problem, verifies, and self-corrects before answering.

quiz.question: What does 'inference-time compute' add to the traditional 'scale up training' approach to improving AI capabilities?

options.text: It lets developers retrain the model on new data after every user query
It lets an already-trained model spend variable amounts of computation per query, deliberating longer on harder problems
It compresses the model's parameters so inference runs faster on small devices
It distributes training across more GPUs to produce bigger models

quiz.explanation: The notes compare training-time compute to education and inference-time compute to time spent on a specific task: previously intractable problems become solvable by letting an existing model think longer, not by training a bigger one.

quiz.question: Mistral Large 3 has 675 billion total parameters but activates only about 41 billion per token. According to the notes, why does this Mixture-of-Experts design matter for researchers?

options.text: It guarantees the model never makes factual errors, since each expert verifies the others
It increases the context window proportionally to the total parameter count
It delivers the knowledge capacity of a massive model at the inference cost of a much smaller one, even making local deployment feasible
It removes the need for reinforcement learning during training

quiz.explanation: Like a hospital routing patients to specialists, an MoE model engages only the relevant experts per token, which underlies DeepSeek's roughly 5-10% pricing and lets sparse open models run on high-end workstations instead of data centers.
